title: Cardiogenic Shock post-STEMI
chief_complaint: Severe crushing chest pain for 4 hours. Now he's confused, cold, and clammy. Blood pressure is barely palpable.

physical_exam.General: Confused (GCS 13), pale, diaphoretic, cold extremities, mottled skin
physical_exam.Cardiac: Tachycardia, S3 gallop, new holosystolic murmur at apex, distant heart sounds
physical_exam.Lungs: Diffuse crackles throughout both lung fields (flash pulmonary edema)
physical_exam.Extremities: Cold, mottled, capillary refill >5 seconds, thready pulses

hidden_data.ecg: Sinus tachycardia 118 bpm. ST elevation 4mm in V1-V6, I, aVL (extensive anterior STEMI). ST depression in II, III, aVF (reciprocal changes). Pathologic Q waves forming in anterior leads.
hidden_data.echo_stat: Severely reduced LV function (EF 20%). Anterior, septal, and apical akinesis. Inferior and lateral segments hyperdynamic. Severe functional mitral regurgitation. Moderate RV dysfunction.
hidden_data.cxr: Pulmonary edema (bilateral alveolar infiltrates). Cardiomegaly. Kerley B lines.
hidden_data.abg: pH 7.18, PaCO2 48, PaO2 52, HCO3 15, SaO2 82%. Severe metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation failure.
hidden_data.cmp: Creatinine 2.1 (AKI), BUN 45, K+ 5.6, AST 250, ALT 180 (shock liver)
hidden_data.cath: 100% proximal LAD occlusion (culprit). 70% RCA stenosis, 60% LCx stenosis.
hidden_data.hemodynamics: Cardiac Index 1.6 L/min/m², PCWP 32 mmHg, Systemic Vascular Resistance 1800, SvO2 45% (severe cardiogenic shock)

correct_diagnosis: Extensive Anterior STEMI with Cardiogenic Shock (SCAI Stage D - Deteriorating), Acute Pulmonary Edema, and Multi-Organ Dysfunction
key_learning_points: Cardiogenic shock = tissue hypoperfusion due to cardiac pump failure: SBP <90 for >30 min OR vasopressors needed + PCWP >15 + CI <2.2 L/min/m²
SCAI Shock Classification: A (At risk), B (Beginning), C (Classic), D (Deteriorating), E (Extremis). This patient is Stage D: requiring multiple vasopressors, worsening acidosis and lactate
Mortality in cardiogenic shock: 40-60% despite aggressive treatment. Early revascularization (PCI within 90 minutes) is the ONLY intervention proven to reduce mortality (SHOCK Trial)

Hemodynamic support: Norepinephrine (first-line vasopressor), Dobutamine or Milrinone (inotropes). Mechanical support: IABP (no mortality benefit, IABP-SHOCK II), Impella, or VA-ECMO for refractory shock
Right heart catheterization guides management: Low CI + high PCWP = LV failure. Norepinephrine for vasoplegia, diuretics/ultrafiltration for volume overload

patient_responses.EMS: Chest pain started 4 hours ago. Patient thought it was indigestion. Called 911 when he collapsed. Aspirin 324mg given by EMS. Vitals deteriorating en route.
patient_responses.wife: He was fine this morning. He complained of heartburn after breakfast. At noon he collapsed getting up from his chair. He's never had heart problems before.
patient_responses.risk: He smokes 2 packs a day for 40 years. High blood pressure but doesn't take medications. Strong family history of heart disease.

feedback.correct_message: Excellent assessment of a devastating condition. You correctly identified cardiogenic shock from massive anterior STEMI with SCAI Stage D severity (multi-organ failure, rising lactate, worsening acidosis). Urgent revascularization of the LAD is the only chance for survival. Mechanical circulatory support (Impella/VA-ECMO) should be considered given refractory shock.
feedback.attending_note: Emergency PCI to LAD (door-to-balloon <90 min). Norepinephrine IV for MAP >65. Dobutamine 5mcg/kg/min for inotropy. Intubate for respiratory failure and airway protection. Impella CP for LV unloading if available (reduces myocardial work while maintaining cardiac output). Avoid beta-blockers acutely. IV Furosemide for pulmonary edema once perfusing. Serial lactate, ABG, and cardiac index to monitor response. ICU admission. If no improvement with PCI + mechanical support: VA-ECMO as bridge to recovery or transplant.
